brand.name: KitchenAid
brand.descriptors_json: {"description":"KitchenAid is a leading kitchen appliance brand that inspires home cooks and professional chefs alike. Since 1919, the company designs and sells a wide range of countertop and major appliances—including stand mixers, blenders, food processors, ovens, refrigerators, dishwashers, and more—crafted to combine performance, durability, and style. KitchenAid’s products aim to unlock culinary creativity, offering innovative features, financing options, and free returns. The brand also fosters a community of makers, encouraging users to share recipes and tips while providing support through its online store and customer service.","slogan":"Bringing culinary inspiration to life since 1919."}

instagram.biography: For all who love to cook, craft, bake & brew, we exist to open a world of possibility in the kitchen.

body.text:
I forgot the order of my own grandmother's pound cake recipe last Thursday at 2:14 in the afternoon and I sat down on my kitchen floor and cried for 40 minutes.

I've made that cake 200 times.

I'm not exaggerating. I've made it for every birthday in this family since 1987. My son's birthdays. My daughter's birthdays. My husband Carl's birthdays. My grandkids. My in laws. My church. My neighbors when their dogs died. My friend Diane after her hysterectomy in 2003.

200 times. At least.

Cream the butter and the sugar for 8 minutes until it's white and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time. Sift the flour with the salt and the baking powder. Add it in three parts, alternating with the buttermilk, starting and ending with the flour. Fold in the vanilla and the lemon zest at the end.

I could do it with my eyes closed. I have done it with my eyes closed, on a dare, at my sister's bridal shower in 1991.

Last Thursday, I creamed the butter and the sugar. I added the eggs. I picked up the flour. I stood there with the bag in my hand.

And I couldn't remember if I sifted it first or added it straight.

I stood there for what felt like 5 minutes but was probably 30 seconds. The Kitchenaid was still running. I could hear it humming. The afternoon sun was coming through my kitchen window, the one that faces the dogwood tree my husband planted in 2001.

I put the flour bag down on the counter.

I sat down on the kitchen floor with my back against the cabinets.

And I cried like I haven't cried since my mother died in 2014.

I'm Maxine Whitfield. I'm 58 years old. I live in a 1960s ranch on Pulaski Avenue in Athens, Georgia, two blocks from the high school where I taught English Literature for 26 years.

My grandson Marcus turns 6 next Saturday.

That cake was for him.

I want to tell you who I was before this started.

I was the woman in our family who remembered everything. Every birthday. Every anniversary. Every name of every cousin's new baby. The first day of school for all 4 of my grandkids. The order of the songs at my own wedding in 1989.

I was the woman my friends called when they couldn't remember the name of the actor in that movie they saw 6 years ago. I was the woman who taught Beowulf and Chaucer to 17 year olds for almost 3 decades, and I never used notes. The poem was just in me.

I won the spelling bee in my district 3 years in a row in middle school. My maiden name is on a plaque in the gymnasium of W.E.B. DuBois Middle School in Macon, Georgia.

That woman.

That's who I was.

That's who I have been my whole life, all the way up to about 2 years ago.

It started small. The way these things always start small.

I was at the grocery store on Alps Road, the Kroger, in the spring of 2023. I was standing in the produce section holding a bag of yellow onions and I couldn't remember why I needed them. I stood there for 4 or 5 minutes trying to retrace my morning. I'd written a list. The list was at home on the kitchen counter.

I put the onions down and I went home.

When I got home, I looked at the list. Onions for the gumbo I was making that night.

I laughed about it. I told Carl over dinner. He laughed too. He said "welcome to my world, baby." He's 60. He's been losing his keys since the Carter administration. We laughed.

But it kept happening.

I'd walk into a room and stand there. I'd open the refrigerator and forget what I was looking for. I'd start a sentence in the middle of telling Diane a story and I'd just stop. The next word wouldn't come. I'd stand there with my mouth open and Diane would gently feed me the word and I'd thank her and move on.

I started writing things down.

I bought a planner from the Target on Atlanta Highway in August of 2023. A real one. Spiral bound. With pages for each week. I started writing every single thing I had to do that day, including things like "call Carl to remind him about the trash" and "take out the chicken to thaw."

The planner became my brain.

If something wasn't in the planner, it didn't exist.

I lost the planner once, in October, and I sat in my car in the Publix parking lot for 20 minutes trying to remember what I was supposed to do that day.

I want to tell you what I was telling myself this whole time.

I was telling myself it was just menopause. I was telling myself it was just stress. I was telling myself it was just the new cholesterol medicine my doctor put me on after my numbers came back high in 2022. I was telling myself it was just because I'd retired from teaching in 2021 and I wasn't using my brain the way I used to.

I was telling myself a lot of things.

Carl was telling me the same things. So was Diane. So was my son Andre, who lives in Atlanta and calls every Sunday. So was my daughter Jasmine, who lives in Decatur with the grandkids.

"Mom, you're fine. You taught Beowulf for 26 years. Your brain is fine."

But here's the thing. I was the one inside my head. I was the one who knew.

I was the one who couldn't follow the recipe I'd made 200 times.

I went to my doctor in November. Dr. Wilson. Black woman, my age, sharp as a knife. I love her. I've been seeing her since 2009.

She listened. She did some blood work. She did one of those little memory screening tests where they ask you to remember 3 words and then ask you again 5 minutes later.

I remembered all 3 words.

She said my screening was normal. She said it was probably perimenopause settling in. She said she could refer me to a neurologist if I wanted, but she didn't think it was necessary.

I didn't take the referral.

I went home and I told myself I was being dramatic.

I went home and I bought a bigger planner.

I want to fast forward to last Thursday.

Last Thursday, I was making the pound cake for Marcus.

The kitchen smelled like butter and sugar. The Kitchenaid was humming. The dogwood tree was in bloom outside the window. I had a cup of cold coffee on the counter from breakfast that I kept meaning to dump.

I'd made the cake the day before. The first one. The cream of tartar and baking soda one. The one with the lemon glaze. Marcus loves the lemon glaze.

I'd brought it to my husband at the dinner table, because Carl always tastes the first one to make sure it's right. It's our ritual. It's been our ritual for 36 years.

He took a bite.

He chewed.

He looked up at me and he said, very gently, "Max, baby. There's no salt in this."

I had forgotten the salt.

I had been creaming butter and sugar and sifting flour for that recipe for 38 years and I had never once forgotten the salt.

I stood there at the dinner table looking at Carl looking at me and something in my chest got very, very cold.

I went back into the kitchen. I started over the next morning.

That's the cake I was making at 2:14 PM last Thursday.

The redo.

That's the cake I was 4 ingredients into when I forgot whether I sifted the flour first or added it straight.

That's why I was sitting on my kitchen floor crying.

I sat there until 2:54.

Carl came home from his Thursday afternoon golf game with his brother. He found me on the floor. He didn't say anything. He just sat down next to me on the linoleum and put his arm around me.

He said "Max, what is going on."

I told him.

I told him about the onions. I told him about the planner. I told him about the words that wouldn't come. I told him about the salt the night before.

I told him I was scared.

I told him I was scared I was disappearing in pieces, and that the woman who taught Beowulf for 26 years and remembered every birthday in our family was already half gone, and that one day soon I wouldn't even remember that I used to be her.

Carl held me.

He didn't tell me I was being dramatic.

He didn't tell me it was menopause.

He didn't tell me my brain was fine because I taught Beowulf.

He just held me.

And then he said something I didn't expect.

He said, "Max, my sister Phyllis went through this in 2019. Don't you remember? She was forgetting things. She was getting scared. And then she found that thing she takes."

I looked up at him.

Phyllis is Carl's older sister. She's 62. She lives in Augusta. She used to be a nurse at the VA. She had been struggling for about 18 months back then with what she was calling "the fog." I remembered her telling me about it at Thanksgiving in 2018. She said she was forgetting medication names. She said she'd looked at a chart at work and her own handwriting hadn't made sense to her.

I hadn't seen her in 4 years. We don't get up to Augusta the way we used to.

I called her that night.

Phyllis answered on the second ring. She sounded like Phyllis. Sharp. Loud. Laughing at something her dog did before she even said hello.

I told her what was happening.

She got quiet.

And then she said, "Maxie, you remember when I was going through the fog?"

I said I remembered.

She said, "I want you to listen to me, because I tried to tell my doctor about it for 2 years and she kept telling me it was menopause and stress and getting older. And I almost just accepted that. I almost just bought a bigger planner."

She said, "Then my friend Loretta from church told me about something her son had been reading about. A fruit. From India. Called Amla."

I'd never heard the word.

I'm an English teacher. I read everything. I've read fruit names in dozens of languages. I'd never heard Amla.

She said Loretta's son was a research librarian at Fort Valley State and he'd been deep in studies about something called oxidative stress and inflammation in the brain. About how a lot of what we call "brain fog" and "memory slipping" in middle age isn't necessarily the start of something terrible. It's often the buildup of damage at the cellular level. Damage that interferes with how the cells in our brains and our nervous systems do their job.

She said Loretta gave her a bottle. She said she didn't expect anything.

She said it took about 5 weeks.

She said she's been on it for 4 years now and the fog never came back.

I sat on the edge of my bed with the phone in my hand and I cried again.

I want to tell you what I learned, after I got off the phone with Phyllis.

Because I went to the laptop and I started reading until 1 in the morning. Carl brought me tea. Then he brought me crackers. Then he went to bed and told me to come find him when I was ready.

I'm not going to pretend I understood every word of every study I read. I'm an English teacher, not a biochemist. But you give me enough text and I can find the pattern. That's the whole job of literary analysis. You read enough and you start seeing what the text is doing.

And the pattern that started forming as I read was something I had never been told before.

The cells in your brain and your nervous system are some of the most demanding cells in your body. They burn through enormous amounts of energy. They run all day, every day, with no breaks. Every thought you have. Every memory you pull up. Every word you reach for. All of it is happening at the cellular level, and all of it requires power.

That power gets made inside little structures in every cell called mitochondria. Mitochondria are like tiny power plants. They turn what you eat and breathe into the fuel your cells run on.

But mitochondria don't run clean forever. As we get older, and as we live our lives, the mitochondria in our cells start to wear down. Years of inflammation, years of stress, years of processed food, years of just being alive in 2026, all of it builds up.

When mitochondria don't run clean, two things happen.

One, they produce less power. The cells they live in don't have what they need to do their job. In your brain, that can show up as fog. As reaching for a word that won't come. As forgetting a recipe you've made 200 times.

Two, they produce more waste. A specific kind of waste called oxidative stress. Free radicals that bounce around and damage things inside the cell. In your brain, that damage adds up. It interferes with how cells communicate. It makes the fog worse.

It's a cycle.

Less power, more damage, less power, more damage.

And nothing in my whole long life of pamphlets and doctor's visits and well-woman exams had ever told me about it.

I read until I couldn't read anymore.

And the question I kept coming back to was the simplest one.

If the mitochondria are running dirty, what helps them run cleaner?

If the brain cells are getting hit with damage, what helps protect them?

That's where Amla came back into the picture.

Amla is a small green fruit, about the size of a golf ball, that grows on a tree native to India. They've been using it in their traditional medicine for over 3,000 years. The old translation of its name is something like "the nurse," because of how broadly it supports the body.

But what stopped me, sitting at my kitchen table at 11:30 at night, was what the research has been finding about it at the cellular level.

Amla is loaded with compounds called emblicanins, which research suggests are some of the most potent natural antioxidants ever measured. Antioxidants are molecules that can help neutralize the free radicals that build up as oxidative stress. They help clean up the damage before it accumulates.

Amla has also been associated with activating something called AMPK. AMPK is one of the master switches inside your cells. When AMPK is active, it tells your cells to clean up. To clear out damaged components. To support the mitochondria. To run cleaner.

And research suggests Amla's compounds may help support how cells throughout the body, including in the brain and nervous system, manage inflammation and protect themselves from oxidative damage.

When the cells in your brain are better supported, they can do their job. They can communicate. They can pull up the word. They can remember the order of the recipe. They can be the woman who taught Beowulf for 26 years, instead of the woman sitting on a kitchen floor crying.

I'm not telling you Amla is a miracle. I'm an English teacher. I don't believe in miracles. I believe in small, careful, well chosen words and patient sentences that build on each other.

But for the first time in 2 years, I had a frame for what was happening to me that wasn't "you're getting old, buy a bigger planner."

The frame was, the engines in the cells of your brain are running dirty, and there's something that supports them.

I bought a bottle of Alevia Amla that night. From my laptop, on my kitchen table, at 12:47 AM, while Carl snored down the hall.

It came in 4 days. Two capsules a day, with breakfast.

I want to tell you what's happened since, but I want to be careful. I don't want to be the woman on Facebook telling you a fairy tale. I'm a literature teacher. I know fairy tales. They have very specific structures and they are not what is happening in real life.

But here's what I noticed.

The first 3 weeks, almost nothing. I felt a little less foggy in the late afternoon. That was it. I almost gave up. I called Phyllis. She told me to stay with it.

In the second month, I noticed I'd stopped writing certain things in the planner. The chicken to thaw. The trash reminder for Carl. They just stayed in my head again.

Around week 6, Diane and I had lunch at the Mama's Boy Cafe on Cherry Street. I told her a story about my niece's wedding in 2017. The whole story. Names, dates, the song they walked down the aisle to, the joke the best man told. I told it without stopping. Without reaching. Without that little click in my brain where a word should have been.

When I finished, Diane was looking at me funny.

She said, "Max. Where did you go just now?"

I said, "What do you mean?"

She said, "You sounded like you. You sounded like you used to."

I sat there with my Caesar salad in front of me and I felt my eyes get hot and I just nodded at her.

In the third month, I made my grandmother's pound cake again.

Marcus had a half birthday celebration his mother had cooked up because he was disappointed about something at school. Jasmine called me on a Wednesday and asked if I could make the cake.

I made it. From memory. No planner. No phone notes.

Cream the butter and the sugar for 8 minutes until it's white and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time. Sift the flour with the salt and the baking powder. Add it in three parts, alternating with the buttermilk, starting and ending with the flour. Fold in the vanilla and the lemon zest at the end.

The whole thing.

The salt was in it.

I brought it to the table. Carl took the first bite. He chewed. He looked up at me.

He didn't say anything.

He just nodded.

And I went into the bathroom in the hall and I closed the door and I looked at myself in the mirror.

The woman looking back at me was the woman who taught Beowulf.

She was a little older. She was tired. She had grey at her temples she hadn't bothered to dye in months.

But she was there.

She was all the way there.

I want to tell you about Marcus's actual birthday. Last Saturday.

I made the cake the night before. I made it from memory. The kitchen smelled like butter and sugar and lemon zest and I knew exactly what I was doing every step of the way. I sang along to Anita Baker on the radio while I was sifting the flour.

Marcus came in on Saturday wearing a cape. He's 6. He's at the cape stage. He saw the cake on the counter and he ran to me and he wrapped his arms around my legs and he said, "Grandma. You made me the cake."

I said, "I made you the cake, baby."

He said, "Did you remember to put the lemon on top this time?"

I laughed.

I picked him up. He's getting heavy. I held him on my hip the way I used to hold his mother. I showed him the cake.

I said, "I remembered everything, baby."

He said, "Grandma, you always remember everything."

I said, "That's right. Grandma always remembers."

And I want to say something to you, if you're still reading.

If you're a woman in your 50s or your 60s, and you've been losing words and forgetting why you walked into rooms and quietly buying bigger planners, and you've been telling yourself it's menopause or stress or just getting older, I want you to think about what I learned at my kitchen table that night.

"Just getting older" is sometimes a story we tell ourselves about something that has a different name underneath.

The cells in your brain need power to do their job. The engines in those cells need to run clean. Sometimes they need support.

I'm not your doctor. I'm not telling you anything is wrong with what you're doing. Talk to your doctor. Show her this if you want.

But the thing I wish I'd known 2 years ago, when I stood in the produce section of Kroger holding a bag of yellow onions, is that there are answers out there besides "buy a bigger planner."

Alevia Amla is what worked for me. Two capsules a day, with breakfast. They make it here in the USA from a standardized extract of the Indian gooseberry. They give you 90 days to try it, and if it does nothing for you, you send it back and they refund you. No fight. No questions.

I bought my fifth bottle this morning, before I sat down to write this.

If you've been losing pieces of yourself, and you've been told it's just menopause, I want you to at least look at it.

Because I made the pound cake last Saturday.

I made it from memory.

I held my grandson on my hip and I told him grandma always remembers, and for the first time in 2 years that was true.

If you want to look at it, here's where you can.

P.S. The 90 day guarantee is what made me click the button that night at 12:47 in the morning. I figured if it did nothing in 6 weeks I'd send it back. I never sent it back. I just kept buying more.

P.P.S. I threw out the big planner last Tuesday. I kept the little one in my purse, the one I always used to carry. I write things in it the way I always did. Birthdays. Doctor appointments. Library book due dates. Not "take out the chicken." Not "remind Carl about the trash." Just the things a person writes down.

P.P.P.S. Phyllis came down from Augusta last weekend for Marcus's birthday. We sat on my back porch after the kids went home and we drank sweet tea and we watched the dogwood tree and we didn't say much. We didn't need to. She knew what I was thinking. I knew what she was thinking. That's what sisters in law do, when they've both been somewhere and come back.

P.P.P.P.S. If you have a sister in law or a friend or a cousin who's been quietly buying bigger planners, ask her how she's doing. Really ask. She might be sitting on a kitchen floor and not telling anyone. Phyllis's call to me changed everything. Be that person for someone else.

👉 https://alevia.com/amla/33

snapshot.link_description: "This actually worked, thank you so much." - Emily T.
snapshot.title: Feel Like Yourself Again

cards.body: Give your kitchen a beautifully curated update by personalizing your finish and hardware with the KitchenAid® line of appliances. Black Ore, Juniper, and Printshield™ Stainless Steel finishes act as the ideal foundation for any kitchen design while brass, stainless, bronze, and black ore hardware offer the ideal finishing touch.
